payload.text: A mobile app called Christ life Bweyogerere abbreviated as CLB with room for live streams and google meet for members using the app and a number of bibles available on the web including those in the Luganda version also add a sector that allows the church administrators to post their sermons for review create a sector for people  where the church can stream its music which is under worship harvest and can be streamed on spotify and all available music platforms create sectors called MCs, where different members can chat make calls and create meetups another sector called media team where people in the media team can communicate and  make new changes to the churches devices and show the public how the to run different devices another sector called worship team that allows the worship team to communicate and show the public the songs they will be singing on specific days another sector called dance team allowing the dancers to record and upload their dance sessions and also another sector called kids church with kids only content and kids cartoons that are bible related another sector called admin that allows administrators communicate and plan for the church there should also be a page for offertories and donations where people can donate to the church by filling in their information and using the churches information system there should also be a voice assistant to help users locate some features on the app and make inquries also ceate a sector for adverts were the church can post posters and different informations sending information to the public.

**Considering App Features**

I'm currently fleshing out the core functionalities of "Christ Life Bweyogerere." Top priorities are live streaming (Google Meet integration) for remote participation, and of course, easy Bible access. I'm also thinking about ways to make it mobile-friendly and user-friendly for a diverse congregation.


**Expanding App Capabilities**

I've been brainstorming about enriching the app, prioritizing features. Luganda Bibles are a must-have, alongside a platform for admin sermon reviews. I'm also planning music streaming via platforms like Spotify, and building missional communities with features like chat and calls. The media team will have dedicated communication tools and guides on devices, and the worship team will get setlist support. Further, a kids' church section, donation form, voice assistant, and announcements are on the roadmap.


**Outlining App Structure**

I'm now diving into the structural details. I'm focusing on the component and view breakdowns, and the file layout now seems clear to me. Specifically, I've got the essential navigation, the Home view with adverts, and the distinct views for LiveStream, Bible, Music, and Giving all mapped out. I'm grouping the sub-communities under a 'Community' section, which feels more intuitive. I'm setting up the Gemini Flash 2.5 API for the Voice Assistant for inquiries.


**Designing Voice Integration**

I'm now integrating the voice assistant. The core is straightforward: capture audio (browser API), send text to Gemini (Live API), and speak the response. The Gemini Live API seems the best fit. I'm prioritizing a clean UI: a floating action button will initiate the session.
I'm revisiting the navigation.
The main areas will have a bottom bar.
"Groups" will include all the various sub-teams.


**Developing Team Functionality**

I'm presently building features for different teams, starting with MCs (chat, meetups), media (tutorials, chat), worship (setlists), dance (uploads, gallery), and admin (sermon posting, planning chat). I've decided to adopt a vibrant Purple/Indigo color scheme, aiming for a modern and clean aesthetic. I am continuing to keep mobile-first design principles in mind, focusing on flex columns, a bottom navigation, and touch-friendly targets. I'm now creating `metadata.json`, `index.html`, and `types.ts`. I also need to make sure permissions are set up for camera and microphone access.


**Organizing App Files**

I'm presently building out the app's file structure. I've designed `enums.ts` for views and content types, and I've got `constants.ts` populated with mock data for sermons and songs. The `services/geminiLive.ts` file is under development with Gemini Live API implementation. I'm focusing on the app's components, including the Layout, Bottom Navigation, and Voice Assistant. Core views are being built for Home, Bible, Groups, Giving, and Music. I'm aware of the "Infinite Loop" and will steer clear.


**Refining Team Integration**

I'm expanding team-specific functionality, concentrating on distinct interfaces. The Dance team will have a video grid and an upload option. For the Media team, I'm integrating a wiki-style list and a chat. Lastly, the Admin team will get a full-featured dashboard. I'm prioritizing building `geminiLive.ts` to implement the voice assistant, and I'll carefully adapt the instructions provided to handle audio streaming within the `useGeminiLive` hook.


**Constructing App Components**

I'm now getting into the meat of app development, and I've started building the "SectorView" which will streamline team functionality. I'm prioritizing flexibility and reusability. I'm also now planning out how to handle the "Google Meet" simulation, as direct API integration is beyond scope. I'm focusing on making the app visually appealing and easy to navigate for diverse users. I've switched to `views/SectorView.tsx` now.

payload.text:
use node.js to help me build the backend for the above mobile app
using 6006741566 as the bank account number for giving in donations,https://us06web.zoom.us/j/84928699926?pwd=motRlw9xALYfwiQutViLFYOBB4NVb8.1

**Updating Donation Details**

I'm focusing on incorporating the new bank account number, 60067415, into the Giving.tsx component. This is the initial step in updating the app's donation process.


**Implementing Zoom Integration**

I'm now integrating the Zoom link into the `Home.tsx` component, and I am reviewing other areas for any necessary additions. The link is `https://us06web.zoom.us/j/84928699926?pwd=motRlw9xALYfwiQutViLFYOBB4NVb8.1`. I will then move on to the backend work.



**Revising Scope and Strategy**

I'm adapting my approach to match the user's focus on changing the application. I will focus on a simulated API service within the client-side app. Now, I am updating Giving.tsx to show the new bank account number 6006741566 and Home.tsx to replace the Google Meet link with the provided Zoom link. I'll create `services/api.ts` to simulate backend endpoints like donations and announcements, and refine the meeting logic for Zoom.


**Clarifying Backend Simulation**

I'm now clarifying the nature of the "backend" for this project. Although I can create a Node.js `server.ts` or `server.js` file, my primary focus will be simulating backend interactions via a mock API service in `services/api.ts`. I'm updating `Home.tsx`, `Giving.tsx`, and `constants.ts`, and refining the "Join Zoom Meeting" button and bank transfer display. The Giving screen will have a new bank transfer card, and the Home screen reflects Zoom integration.


**Defining Architectural Approach**

I'm now consolidating the architectural design, integrating the backend simulation and external server representation. I'm focusing on defining the approach. I'll update `constants.ts` with the new `ZOOM_LINK` and `BANK_ACCOUNT`, then update `views/Home.tsx` to handle the Zoom link and `views/Giving.tsx` for the bank account details. I'm also including a `server.ts` file demonstrating a rudimentary Node.js backend. My next step involves detailing the simulated backend services.

I will update the CLB app to include your specific Zoom link for meetings and the bank account number for donations. I'll also add a `server.ts` file to serve as a high-quality Node.js/Express backend foundation for your application.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **Zoom Integration**:
    *   **Location**: `Home.tsx` and the `LIVE_STREAM` view.
    *   **Change**: Replaced generic Google Meet links with the provided Zoom meeting link (`https://us06web.zoom.us/j/84928699926...`).
    *   **Appearance**: A dedicated "Join Zoom Meeting" button with a brand-consistent icon.

2.  **Donation & Giving**:
    *   **Location**: `Giving.tsx`.
    *   **Change**: Added a prominent "Bank Transfer" section displaying the account number **6006741566**.
    *   **Appearance**: A clean, copyable card showing the bank details, alongside the existing Mobile Money options.

3.  **Node.js Backend (server.ts)**:
    *   **Implementation**: A robust Express.js server boilerplate.
    *   **Features**: Endpoints for handling donation confirmations, fetching church announcements, and managing community messages.
    *   **Structure**: Uses TypeScript for type safety, matching the frontend's models.

4.  **Frontend API Service**:
    *   **New File**: `services/api.ts`.
    *   **Purpose**: Acts as the bridge between the React frontend and the new Node.js backend.

### Implementation

payload.text:
Fix the following errors:

```
Gemini Live Error
Error: Network error
```

**Troubleshooting the Integration**

I'm currently focused on the "Network error" within the CLB app's Gemini Live integration. Examining the `services/geminiLive.ts` code, the model parameter passed to `ai.live.connect` appears to be 'gemini-2.5-flash-native'. I need to determine if this model is the cause of the network error.


**Refining the Model Parameter**

I've determined the model parameter is the likely culprit, as the `09-2025` version might be invalid per the `@google/genai` guidelines. The correct model for real-time audio conversations is `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`, according to documentation. I'm now updating the code to use the approved model.


**Confirming the Configuration**

I'm now certain the model name was the primary issue, and I have updated the configuration object directly within the `ai.live.connect` call. I confirmed my changes mirror the example provided by the documentation, so now I'm watching to see if this fixes the issue. I am also looking at other parameters now.


**Identifying Browser-Server Conflict**

I've just uncovered a potential clash: the import map includes server-side libraries like `express`, which won't function in the browser environment. The user's request for a Node.js backend might be the source of this problem. My focus is on resolving this integration issue by reviewing the codebase, since `express` should not be included.


**Analyzing Potential Causes**

I've been analyzing the code for potential causes of the network error. I checked that the `GoogleGenAI` initialization and `voiceConfig` are as instructed. The `session.sendRealtimeInput` call looks correct, including the `media` property. Now, I'm focusing on the model name and API key, and I will be looking closely at the `onmessage` handler.


**Validating Encoding Methods**

I am now validating the encoding methods used. I've confirmed that my implementation of `arrayBufferToBase64` correctly follows the instructions, and is a direct analog of the provided example of `encode`. I've checked the usage of  `message.serverContent?.modelTurn?.parts?.[0]?.inlineData?.data` and it's being used as expected to access the base64 encoded data. I am continuing to check for possible issues.


**Examining Sample Rates**

I'm now diving into the audio sample rates. I've validated that my `arrayBufferToBase64` function mirrors the example `encode` function. The code also initiates `sendRealtimeInput` after `live.connect` resolves, which avoids race conditions. I'm scrutinizing the audio sample rates used in the example and the current setup, suspecting a possible discrepancy.


**Checking Version Support**

I'm now investigating potential version compatibility issues. The suggested model `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-09-2025` may be a problem. The instructions list `'gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025'` as the supported model. Also, I'm verifying the `onaudioprocess` handler.


**Pinpointing the Root Cause**

I'm now honing in on the error's source. I'm carefully reviewing the `onmessage` handler to ensure it correctly accesses the audio data at index `0`. Simultaneously, I'm verifying the `ai.live.connect` configuration. I'm also now considering the possibility of a network connection issue stemming from the frontend's attempt to contact a non-existent local server.


**Addressing the Gemini Connection**

I'm now zeroing in on the Gemini API connection issue, specifically the `onerror` callback within `services/geminiLive.ts`. I'm going to update the model to `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025` and ensure that the `config` object in `ai.live.connect` is precisely as instructed. I will continue to investigate, and I'm ensuring `process.env.API_KEY` is being correctly utilized.


**Fixing the `geminiLive.ts`**

I'm now zeroing in on resolving the Gemini connection issues within `services/geminiLive.ts`, and am verifying the `config` object in `ai.live.connect` matches the instructions. It looks like the model needs to be updated. The correct value is `'gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025'`. I am focusing on this first.


**Verifying the Connection Parameters**

I'm now carefully re-examining the `ai.live.connect` call signature and configuration. The "Network error" is frequently rooted in invalid model names, API keys, or incorrect configuration. While I've double-checked the model name previously, I am now meticulously verifying that the provided API key is valid and correctly configured, and that I'm implementing the callbacks as suggested, and that my region is supported. My aim is to make certain all aspects align perfectly with the required setup.


**Verifying the Callbacks**

I'm now carefully re-examining the `ai.live.connect` call, but I must also check `server.ts` where `express` is used. While technically possible with `esm.sh`, `express` won't run in a standard browser environment. However, since the error is a "Gemini Live Error", I'm going back to `services/geminiLive.ts`. Now, I will update the model name and helper functions in `services/geminiLive.ts`, while also checking `constants.ts` and the provided account number.


**Refining the Troubleshooting Approach**

I'm now carefully reviewing `services/geminiLive.ts`. I noticed the model is configured as `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-09-2025`. I'll switch it to `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025` per the documentation. I have also reconfirmed that `arrayBufferToBase64` and `convertFloat32ToInt16` are configured as expected. I am making sure the call parameters match the latest instructions.


**Confirming Data Decoding**

I'm now carefully examining the data decoding process within the `onmessage` handler. My earlier assessment of the data decoding logic appears correct, and matches the provided instructions. I am verifying the `sessionPromiseRef.current` assignment, and will now add a check to handle the `API_KEY` being possibly empty. I also confirmed that the models in use match the list of required models. I will continue to investigate.


**Reviewing Audio Processing**

I'm now carefully examining the audio processing pipeline. I've re-confirmed that `convertFloat32ToInt16` is correctly implemented. I also reviewed the use of `sessionPromiseRef.current` for sending data. Since the problem is a network error, I'm checking the `services/geminiLive.ts` code for any additional logic errors or discrepancies that might be causing the error, such as invalid parameters or unexpected network requests.


**Refactoring the Audio Pipeline**

I am now thoroughly reviewing the audio processing pipeline, particularly the `onmessage` handler. I'm scrutinizing `convertFloat32ToInt16` and the use of `sessionPromiseRef.current`, because I'm seeing a possible network error. It seems likely the issue lies within `services/geminiLive.ts`. I will now update the model name and other function details, paying close attention to any edge case logic that might be creating a network error.



**Fixing the Import Map**

I'm now zeroing in on the import map. I see `body-parser` and `cors` are included, which are typical backend packages. Since I'm focusing on the frontend, I should confirm their presence doesn't cause any issues in the browser, but first I will update the model name and other function details in `services/geminiLive.ts`. The error might be a result of the model or a failed WebSocket handshake. I am also making sure `process.env.API_KEY` is being correctly utilized.


**Validating Code Integrity**

I'm now diligently verifying code integrity, starting with the model name, and am updating it to the correct `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`. I'm also reviewing the `onmessage` handler for robustness, aligning it closely with the example and addressing potential API key issues, as well as checking the helper functions. I am also checking import paths. Finally, I will check the `decodeAudioData` implementation to match the example more closely if I see any risk.



**Analyzing Audio Decoding**

I'm now diving into the audio decoding process, because I have made sure that the instructions' `decodeAudioData` implementation is identical to the code. I'm focusing on strengthening the connect function as well, and my previous update had a brief reference to an import map, which I'll revisit later. The model name remains the top priority.

To fix the "Network error" occurring with the Gemini Live API, the most critical update is correcting the model name to `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025` as specified in the latest SDK guidelines. Outdated or incorrect model versions (like the previously used `09-2025`) are the most common cause of WebSocket handshake failures in the Live API.

I have also refined the connection logic to ensure the API key is always fresh and updated the configuration to strictly follow the recommended patterns for the Gemini 2.5 series.

payload.text:
build a server that automaticaly send money to the MTN and Airtel merchant codes give it a USSD dial-up code *165*3# and then input the merchant codes for MTN:726123
and airtel: 4380286

**Considering Mobile Backend Enhancements**

I'm now focused on the backend server for the Christ Life Bweyogerere mobile app. The core is simulating or enabling money transfers via MTN and Airtel merchant accounts. Thinking about the architecture to best support these transactions is the next step.


**Developing Payment Simulation Logic**

I'm now integrating logic for simulated "STK Push" payments in my backend, focusing on MTN and Airtel. Realistically, I can't automatically dial USSD from the app itself due to security restrictions; therefore, I'm opting to simulate this server-side and trigger a user prompt. I will update the frontend with an "Initiate Payment" button and also provide the USSD dial instructions as a fallback.


**Refining Payment Flow**

I'm now refining the simulated payment flow. Instead of attempting direct USSD dialing from the app, I'm focusing on providing clear, step-by-step instructions. The server's new `/api/payments/initiate` endpoint will generate provider-specific USSD strings. I'm updating `Giving.tsx` to prominently display these and add a "Direct Dial" button using the `tel:` URI. Furthermore, I will create a mock payment gateway service to simulate a Flutterwave/Yo! Payments integration.


**Implementing Payment Initiation Logic**

I'm now implementing the payment initiation logic in `server.ts`. It will generate USSD strings for MTN and Airtel merchants. I'm updating `Giving.tsx` to display specific USSD codes and add a "Click to Dial" feature using the `tel:` URI. Also, I will enhance the "Authorize Payment" button to visually simulate the "STK Push" process. My focus is on robust logging of the USSD string.


**Defining App API Endpoints**

Now, I'm setting up REST API endpoints in `server.ts` for announcements and payment initiation. The `/api/announcements` endpoint will fetch mock announcements. The `/api/payments/initiate` endpoint handles the simulated payment process. I'm focusing on validating requests and generating the appropriate USSD strings based on the chosen provider.


**Simulating Payment Confirmations**

I'm now implementing the `/api/donations` endpoint in `server.ts` to simulate payment confirmations via POST requests. This simulates a webhook for the simulated gateway. I will accept amount, phone number, type, and donorName in the request body. I'm focusing on the logic that records this simulated payment.


**Processing Donation Recording Logic**

I'm now focusing on the server-side logic for recording donations. I have implemented the `/api/donations` endpoint in `server.ts` to simulate payment confirmation via POST requests, effectively simulating a webhook. The endpoint accepts the amount, phone number, type, and donorName in the request body. I'm prioritizing robust logging to track recorded payments and ensure proper responses for these simulations. Also, I am finalizing the donation recording with proper status handling.


**Refining Provider Detection**

I'm now refining the provider detection logic within `Giving.tsx`. The goal is more accurate determination of network providers (MTN, Airtel, etc.) based on Ugandan mobile number prefixes, enhancing the user experience. I'm focusing on ensuring it handles all the known prefixes correctly.


**Analyzing Payment Simulation Flow**

I'm presently dissecting the payment simulation flow within `Giving.tsx`. The recent update involves submitting payment initiation requests to a mock server. The code handles form submission, network provider fallback (to MTN), and simulated waiting for user PIN input. I'm focusing on user interface aspects such as copy updates and loading states.


**Verifying Success States**

I'm now integrating a success state and associated UI elements. If the simulated payment is "successful" (i.e. the form is submitted), the UI now displays a checkmark and gratitude message. This is designed to improve the user experience for the demo.


**Improving UI Presentation**

I'm now focusing on UI refinements, particularly for the "Give Again" button. I'm adding a specific class (`"bg-indigo-600 text-white px-10 py-4 rounded-2xl font-black uppercase tracking-widest shadow-xl active:scale-95 transition-transform"`) to improve its visual appeal and provide feedback. I'm also ensuring the overall layout with the header and method toggle is clean and easy to use.


**Styling Method Toggle**

I'm presently focused on the method toggle styling. I'm adding a specific class to the `method` button for visual enhancements, including a rounded-xl appearance. The goal is a more user-friendly interface. I'm also focusing on the state when the method button is pressed.


**Updating Bank Transfer UI**

I'm now integrating a bank transfer section into the UI. I have added a section showing the account number, a copy-to-clipboard button, and supporting text. I'm prioritizing clear presentation of the account information and usability. I'm also ensuring the user interface is intuitive.


**Enhancing USSD Instructions**

I'm now integrating a USSD instruction card in the UI, enhancing clarity for MTN and Airtel payments. I've designed a clear visual structure with provider labels and USSD strings using a distinctive background and icon. The layout focuses on presenting the instructions clearly, including merchant codes. The goal is easy understanding for users.

3.  **Visual Improvements**:

    *   The voice command interface features a pulsing animation to indicate when it's actively listening to the user's instructions.

### Code Updates:

payload.text: build me a server using python that i can use to control the mobile app

**Considering Server Replacement**

I'm currently evaluating replacing or supplementing the existing Node/Express backend for the CLB app. My focus is on a Python-based server. I’m thinking about its compatibility with the React frontend and the Gemini API integration. Replacing the `server.ts` file seems like the main goal, but I'm open to supplemental solutions.


**Developing Python Server Components**

I'm now diving into the specifics. I'm leaning toward a FastAPI implementation for `server.py`, as its async capabilities and type safety seem like a good fit. I've outlined the core features: announcements, payment initiation, and a remote control mechanism. Next up is constructing the basic endpoints and ensuring CORS support for frontend communication. The plan is to start with the `GET /api/announcements` endpoint.


**Defining Control Mechanisms**

I'm now honing in on the remote control functionality. I'm adding an `api/app-state` endpoint (GET/POST) to manage the app's live status. I'll update `services/api.ts` to reflect the new structure, adjusting the base URL as needed. This includes providing a way to start the Python server, and updating the client. I'll also add a "Global State" in an "Admin" section to manage "Force Navigation" or "Global Alert".

I will now build a high-performance Python backend using **FastAPI**. This server will replace the previous Node.js mock server and add specific "Remote Control" capabilities, allowing you to manage announcements, payment status, and even trigger global app states (like forcing a live stream or showing a specific alert) from the backend.

### Specification:

1.  **Framework Choice**: **FastAPI** (Python). It's modern, asynchronous, and provides automatic OpenAPI documentation.
2.  **State Management**: Added a global `app_state` dictionary that acts as the "control center." The mobile app can poll this or react to it to change behavior dynamically.
3.  **Endpoints**:
    *   `GET /api/announcements`: Returns church updates.
    *   `POST /api/payments/initiate`: Handles the logic for MTN/Airtel USSD strings.
    *   `GET /api/control`: The "Command Center" endpoint that the mobile app polls to see if the Admin has triggered a "Force Live" or "Special Message."
    *   `POST /api/control/update`: For you to send commands to the app.
4.  **Frontend Integration**: Updated `services/api.ts` to connect to this new Python architecture.

### Code Updates:

entries.description: Create a Python FastAPI server to handle church logic and remote control of the mobile app.

diffs.replacement:
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional
import time

app = FastAPI(title="CLB Church Backend")

# Enable CORS for the frontend to communicate
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

# --- Data Models ---

class Announcement(BaseModel):
    id: str
    title: str
    description: str
    image: Optional[str] = None

class PaymentRequest(BaseModel):
    amount: str
    phone: str
    provider: str
    purpose: str

class ControlState(BaseModel):
    is_live: bool = False
    global_alert: Optional[str] = None
    force_view: Optional[str] = None # e.g., "LIVE_STREAM", "GIVING"

# --- Mock Database ---

db_announcements = [
    { 
        "id": "sunday-garage", 
        "title": "Sunday Garage", 
        "description": "Join us every Sunday! 1st Service: 9:00AM | 2nd Service: 11:30PM",
        "image": "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1544427920-c49ccfb85579?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=800"
    },
    { 
        "id": "mc-live", 
        "title": "MC Live is ON!", 
        "description": "Every Wednesday at 5:30PM in all Missional Communities.",
        "image": "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1511671782779-c97d3d27a1d4?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=800"
    }
]

# This is the "Control" object that manages the app remotely
current_control_state = ControlState(
    is_live=False,
    global_alert="Welcome to CLB Mobile! God bless you.",
    force_view=None
)

# --- Routes ---

@app.get("/api/announcements", response_model=List[Announcement])
async def get_announcements():
    return db_announcements

@app.get("/api/control")
async def get_control_state():
    """Endpoint for the mobile app to poll for remote commands."""
    return current_control_state

@app.post("/api/control/update")
async def update_control_state(state: ControlState):
    """Admin endpoint to change what the mobile app is doing."""
    global current_control_state
    current_control_state = state
    return {"status": "updated", "state": current_control_state}

@app.post("/api/payments/initiate")
async def initiate_payment(req: PaymentRequest):
    """Handles logic for Mobile Money USSD generation."""
    mtn_merchant = "726123"
    airtel_merchant = "4380286"
    
    ussd = ""
    if req.provider.upper() == "MTN":
        ussd = f"*165*3*{mtn_merchant}*{req.amount}#"
    elif req.provider.upper() == "AIRTEL":
        ussd = f"*185*9*{airtel_merchant}*{req.amount}#"
    else:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Invalid provider")
        
    print(f"[PY-SERVER] Initiating {req.provider} payment for {req.amount} UGX")
    
    return {
        "status": "initiated",
        "transactionId": f"PY-CLB-{int(time.time())}",
        "ussdInstruction": ussd,
        "message": f"Please confirm on your phone or dial {ussd}"
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    # In production, use uvicorn server:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 3001
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=3001)
